self_report.reflection: Substituting t = u^2 reduces the integral to 2∫₀^∞ e^{-a(u² + 1/u²)} du. Writing u² + 1/u² = (u - 1/u)² + 2 pulls out e^{-2a}, and the u ↔ 1/u symmetry gives ∫₀^∞ e^{-a(u-1/u)²}(1+1/u²)du = √(π/a) via v = u - 1/u. Hence the integral equals √(π/a)·e^{-2a}, not e^{-a}.
self_report.notes: The exponent should be -2a because min of t + 1/t on (0,∞) is 2, not 1.
